paragraph: Vorthex & Sons announced Monday the acquisition of Yvette Drenwald's debut literary novel The Curlewen Verdict in a preemptive deal valued at $780,000, brokered by agent Petra Silmund of the Silmund-Haas Literary Agency. The 412-page manuscript, set in a claustrophobic coastal town during a decades-long wrongful-conviction appeal, will publish in March 2027 with an initial print run of 85,000 copies. Drenwald, previously known for her prize-shortlisted short fiction in the Aldremian Review, spent six years researching procedural law and community psychology for the project. Vorthex editor-in-chief Caspar Lorenfeld described the novel as a generational reckoning with institutional failure told through the competing voices of seven witnesses. Translation rights for seventeen languages were sold at the Frankfurt Book Fair to publishers across Europe, South America, and Japan, generating an additional $1.2 million in foreign rights income. Film rights were optioned simultaneously by Dreyden-Lux Productions for $350,000 against a $1.8 million purchase price. Vorthex has committed a marketing budget of $420,000, including a twelve-city author tour and a sustained digital campaign targeting book-club audiences. Pre-orders opened the same day and surpassed 12,000 units within forty-eight hours, prompting a print-run increase to 110,000 ahead of the formal announcement.

paragraph: The Drelm Prize for Fiction announced its 2026 longlist of fourteen titles on Tuesday, led by Halberg Korov's sprawling 587-page historical saga The Meridian of Bones, published by Mendovian House in February at a list price of $29.95. Korov, a Latvian-born novelist now residing in Lisbon, received the largest advance in Mendovian House history — $1.15 million for world rights — when the manuscript was acquired in late 2023 following a seven-publisher auction. The Drelm Prize, administered by the Aldremian Foundation for Letters and carrying a cash award of £60,000, has previously recognized novels by Sonia Fethwick, Arpad Golmar, and Celestine Ouvert. Other longlisted titles include Rosamund Bletch's The Ferryman's Daughter from Quill & Torrent Press and Jonas Prewett's debut Quiet Saltwater from Eschenwald Publishing. The longlist committee, chaired by critic and essayist Norbert Vaine, cited renewed ambition in formally experimental fiction as the defining characteristic of this year's eligible pool. Shortlist announcements are scheduled for the last week of September, with the winner to be revealed at the Aldremian Foundation Gala in Edinburgh on November 14. Mendovian House marketing director Leila Straub confirmed that Korov's North American print run has been expanded to 130,000 in anticipation of award momentum.

paragraph: Eschenwald Publishing confirmed a two-book deal with science-fiction writer Tamsin Ruark for a reported $640,000 for world English rights, with her agent Dolph Vreeland of Vreeland Literary Management negotiating the agreement over six weeks of competitive bidding. The first volume, titled The Solvent Dark, is a 374-page near-future thriller centered on a private intelligence contractor who discovers that her employer has been harvesting memories from comatose patients to populate an artificial governance network. Eschenwald publisher Alara Kindtmann called the manuscript one of the most cinematically constructed science-fiction debuts she had read in fifteen years. The second book, provisionally titled Residue, is scheduled for delivery in autumn 2028. Publication of The Solvent Dark is set for July 2027 with an initial print run of 60,000. German, French, Italian, Spanish, Dutch, and Brazilian Portuguese rights were sold within two weeks of the deal's announcement, collectively generating $510,000. A television adaptation is in early development with Orenbeck Content Group, which paid $175,000 for an eighteen-month option. Eschenwald has allocated $300,000 for marketing, including placement in fifteen airport retail chains and a targeted social-media campaign built around speculative fiction communities on three major platforms.

paragraph: Romance imprint Heliograph Books, a division of Fenwick & Allard, reported its strongest first quarter in eleven years following the breakout commercial performance of Dorienne Calst's novel The Temperature of Silk, which sold 340,000 trade paperback copies and 218,000 digital units in the thirteen weeks following its January 14 publication date. The 308-page novel, acquired for $120,000 in an exclusive submission from agent Miriam Torvald of the Torvald-Blaise Agency, follows a Senegalese-Norwegian textile designer navigating a devastating inheritance dispute while falling into an unexpected partnership with a reclusive maritime architect in Bergen. Calst, who previously published two modestly performing novellas under the pseudonym D. Casterley, credited the breakthrough to a coordinated BookTok strategy orchestrated by Heliograph's digital marketing team. The publisher subsequently signed Calst to a three-book continuation deal for $875,000, covering two sequels and a standalone novel set in a different world entirely. Film rights were sold to Vestholm Pictures for $290,000 outright. The novel has been sold to twenty-two territories, and an audio edition narrated by actress Felara Dunst has accumulated 1.4 million streams across subscription platforms in its first ninety days.

paragraph: Veteran literary agent Szofia Bannerfeld of Bannerfeld & Crowe Associates announced this week that she had placed debut novelist Obinna Tarwell's manuscript The Unfinished Country with Caldervane Press in a deal worth $510,000 for North American rights. The 441-page novel traces four generations of a Nigerian-Welsh family across Lagos, Cardiff, and Halifax, examining how inherited silence reshapes political conviction in each successive generation. Caldervane editor Wren Pfeiffer acquired the book after a competitive auction involving five publishers and described the novel as possessing the rare quality of feeling both deeply personal and genuinely historical at the same time. Publication is set for September 2027, with a first printing of 50,000. Subsidiary rights activity has been robust: translation rights were sold to publishers in eleven countries at the Göteborg Book Fair, yielding $390,000 in aggregate. The Welmsley Prize for Debut Fiction has already named The Unfinished Country to its eligibility watchlist, a distinction that typically increases advance-order activity. Caldervane has budgeted $250,000 for marketing, including placement on the coveted Brentham Books first-look shelf program and a partnership with the Diasporic Readers Collective, which claims a newsletter subscriber base of 480,000.

paragraph: The Orvallen Prize for Crime Fiction revealed its 2026 winner on Thursday evening at a ceremony held in the grand hall of the Gothennrast Literary Institute: Friede Kallenbach's procedural mystery The Witness Margin, published in January by Daggermark Press at a retail price of $27.00. paragraph: Mendovian House announced a significant expansion of its science and nature imprint, Helix Meridian Books, with the signing of marine biologist and science communicator Dr. Cass Felhorn to a two-book deal for $720,000. The first title, The Breathing Dark: Life at the Limit of the Abyss, is a 320-page work of popular science examining bioluminescent ecosystems in the deep ocean and their implications for astrobiology. Dr. Felhorn, represented by Genevra Stiles of Hollowell & Stiles Agency, was the subject of a competitive acquisition process involving four publishers. Helix Meridian publisher Brant Osgood cited the author's exceptional ability to render complex biochemistry accessible to general readers without sacrificing intellectual rigor. The book will be accompanied by a twelve-part podcast co-produced with Meridian Audio and a museum exhibit partnership with the Vellamont Institute of Natural Sciences, which has agreed to display Felhorn's field photography across three locations. Paperback rights were pre-sold to a dozen territories at the London Book Fair for a combined $480,000. The second title remains untitled but is expected to address ocean-atmosphere carbon interactions at geological timescales.

paragraph: Quill & Torrent Press unveiled its autumn 2026 fiction catalogue on Wednesday, headlining with the long-awaited fourth novel from Sonia Fethwick, whose previous title The Pale Inventory spent thirty-one weeks on the Aldremian Bestseller List in 2022. paragraph: Arpad Golmar's memoir The Cartography of Blame, published by Ardentine Books in March at $28.00, has been named winner of the Castebrook Prize for Narrative Nonfiction, earning Golmar a cash award of $30,000 and a significant boost in sales. The 388-page memoir chronicles Golmar's adolescence in a disintegrating small-town Hungarian community following the collapse of his family's agricultural cooperative, and his unlikely path to becoming a celebrated novelist in the English-speaking world despite arriving in London at twenty-two without the language. Ardentine editor-in-chief Nora Velthuijs described the acquisition process as one of the most emotionally charged of her career; she acquired the book for $340,000 after reading the manuscript in a single overnight session. Agent Yusuf Brandel of Brandel Associates secured the deal. Following the prize announcement, Ardentine ordered an emergency print run of 90,000 copies to supplement the 55,000 already in distribution. Film rights were sold to Kessmer Productions the same afternoon for $275,000. The memoir has been sold in nineteen territories, with the German-language rights alone commanding $120,000. Golmar is currently at work on his fifth novel, also under contract with Ardentine.

paragraph: The Welmsley Prize for Debut Fiction announced its 2026 shortlist of six novels on Monday, with Obinna Tarwell's The Unfinished Country joined by Celestine Ouvert's The Phosphorescent Hours, Mira Dunthorpe's The Ochre Registry from Blackwood Meridian Press, Seun Adegbite's A Thousand Ordinary Rivers from Vorthex & Sons, Zara Kowalski's Ferrous from Harpswell & Glass, and Luuk de Mond's The Architect of Small Defeats from Eschenwald Publishing. The Welmsley Prize, founded in 1998 and carrying a cash award of £35,000, is widely considered the most reliable predictor of long-term literary reputation among debut novelists. This year's judging panel is chaired by novelist and academic Bram Veldthorst and includes critic Amara Oluwaseun and publisher Gilda Pfennig. Combined pre-shortlist sales of the six titles total approximately 280,000 copies. Industry analysts noted an unusually international shortlist, with authors from seven countries of origin, reflecting what Bookseller trade journal described as a meaningful shift in the prize's historical geographic emphasis. The winner will be announced at a ceremony in London on October 21, 2026. All six publishers have confirmed accelerated print runs in anticipation of the sales uplift typically associated with a Welmsley shortlisting.

paragraph: Harpswell & Glass confirmed the acquisition of a sweeping science-fiction epic from author Davan Reisch for $1.05 million for world English rights, the largest single-book deal in the publisher's thirty-two-year history. The novel, titled The Covelline Succession, runs to 712 pages and depicts the political collapse of a generation-ship civilization during the final years of a four-hundred-year voyage to a distant stellar system. Reisch, previously published by a smaller independent imprint and known for the Yeldrane stories serialized online, was represented by agent Callum Dreed of Ferris & Dreed Associates. Harpswell editorial director Susanne Vanderholm described the acquisition as a statement of intent about the publisher's commitment to ambitious, large-scale speculative fiction. Publication is planned for August 2027. A first print run of 75,000 has been announced, alongside a marketing budget of $350,000 and a partnership with the Cosmographic Society of North America for a twelve-event science-meets-fiction lecture series. French, German, Spanish, Polish, and Japanese rights were sold in the week following the announcement for a combined $720,000. Reisch has delivered an outline for a companion novel set one century before the events of The Covelline Succession.

paragraph: Caldervane Press announced on Friday the promotion of senior editor Rena Polsted to Publisher of its literary fiction division, replacing founding publisher Gottfried Haas who is retiring after twenty-eight years with the company. Polsted, who joined Caldervane as an editorial assistant in 2012, has been responsible for acquiring and editing fourteen prize-nominated titles during her tenure, including three Drelm Prize finalists and one Castebrook Prize winner. Under her editorial leadership, Caldervane's literary fiction list has grown from annual revenues of $4.2 million to $18.7 million. Her first announcement in the publisher role is the acquisition of Vera Lindqvist's third novel, The Cormorant Season, a 430-page work of literary fiction set in the Swedish fishing industry during the 1970s, for $580,000 in a deal brokered by agent Helena Brant of Nordic Rights Literary. Caldervane plans a simultaneous English-language release in North America and the United Kingdom in April 2028, with a combined first printing of 65,000. Swedish-language co-publication rights were sold to Brunnmark Förlag for $110,000. Polsted stated that she intends to maintain Caldervane's reputation for long-view investment in debut authors while expanding the press's commercial footprint in genre-adjacent literary fiction.

paragraph: Blackwood Meridian Press reported that Mira Dunthorpe's sophomore novel The Glass Meridian, the follow-up to the Welmsley-shortlisted The Ochre Registry, has been acquired for $820,000, more than quadrupling the $195,000 advance paid for her debut. The novel has been pre-sold in eighteen territories.

paragraph: The Aldremian Foundation for Letters announced a $2 million endowment to create the Aldremian Prize for Translated Fiction, the first major English-language award exclusively for works in translation from any language. The prize will carry a cash award of £80,000, to be split equally between the original author and the translator, and will be awarded annually beginning with the 2028 cycle. The Foundation's director, Margret Vollsen, stated that the initiative responds to persistent data showing that works in translation represent fewer than four percent of fiction titles acquired by major English-language publishers despite comprising the majority of globally acclaimed literature. The prize will be administered by a rotating panel of five judges including at least two translators and one publisher with a dedicated translation program. Submissions will be open to any work originally written in a language other than English and published in English translation between January and December of the eligible year. Applications from publishers large and small will be accepted. The Foundation has additionally committed $500,000 in development grants over five years to support emerging translators from underrepresented language pairs, with the first call for applications opening in September 2026.

paragraph: Vorthex & Sons reported that Seun Adegbite's debut novel A Thousand Ordinary Rivers has surpassed 200,000 copies sold in its first five months of publication, making it the fastest-selling debut in the publisher's eighty-three-year history. paragraph: Eschenwald Publishing posted a 41 percent increase in net revenue for fiscal year 2025, reaching $67.3 million, driven primarily by the sustained performance of its science-fiction and speculative-fiction backlist and the unexpected breakout of two debut novels acquired for under $200,000 each. Publisher Alara Kindtmann attributed the growth to a distribution restructuring completed in late 2024 that eliminated a costly wholesaler relationship and allowed the company to capture a higher margin per unit. The company's bestselling title of the year was Tamsin Ruark's The Solvent Dark, which generated $11.2 million in revenue across all formats despite not being published until the year's final quarter. Eschenwald also benefited from a backlist resurgence of Davan Reisch's Yeldrane novellas following the announcement of the author's major deal with Harpswell & Glass, which drove renewed interest in titles Eschenwald had originally published. Foreign rights revenue increased by 28 percent to $9.4 million. The company announced plans to expand its editorial staff from fourteen to nineteen, with three of the new positions dedicated to acquiring international literary fiction and two focused on expanding its young adult speculative fiction program, which was launched in 2023 and has already produced one title that sold more than 180,000 copies.

paragraph: The Ferncastle Award for Children's and Young Adult Literature announced that the 2026 prize for Young Adult Fiction has been awarded to Zinnia Ashworth for The Gravity of Small Things, a 316-page novel published by Larkspur Books exploring the experience of a sixteen-year-old amateur astronomer navigating her parents' separation alongside the discovery of what may be an undocumented dwarf planet. paragraph: Ardentine Books announced a landmark deal with historian and public intellectual Professor Lavinia Osei for a three-book history of African governance systems from pre-colonial origins to contemporary constitutional frameworks, titled provisionally the Foundations trilogy. Volumes two and three are under contract for delivery in 2029 and 2031 respectively. North American educational rights have been separately negotiated with university presses in four states. The work has attracted early translation interest from publishers in France, Germany, Japan, and Brazil. Osei, professor of constitutional history at Tambergate University, has previously published two well-regarded academic monographs but Before the Map will be her first work aimed at a general audience.

paragraph: Fenwick & Allard disclosed that it has restructured its imprint portfolio, merging the underperforming literary fiction division Aldgate Press with its commercial fiction imprint Copperfield Books to create a unified adult fiction division to be called Allard Fiction. The reorganization, effective January 1, 2027, will result in the elimination of four editorial positions through a combination of redundancies and attrition. Publisher Deanna Forsberg will lead the new combined division, which will maintain a combined annual list of approximately forty-five titles, compared to a combined fifty-eight under the previous structure. Fenwick & Allard chief executive Pieter Gransen stated that the merger reflects the commercial reality that the boundary between literary and commercial fiction has become increasingly porous, and that a unified acquisition and marketing strategy serves both author and reader more effectively. Authors currently contracted under both imprints will retain their existing agreements. Fenwick & Allard reported consolidated revenues of $124 million for fiscal 2025, a decline of six percent from the prior year attributed to a weaker than expected performance by two anticipated bestsellers and increased returns from retail partners adjusting inventory levels. The company's audiobook and digital divisions grew by 22 and 14 percent respectively.

paragraph: The Brentham Books chain, operator of 412 retail locations across North America, announced the expansion of its proprietary recommendation program Brentham Selects to include a dedicated translated fiction shelf in all stores with more than 5,000 square feet of floor space, affecting approximately 280 locations. Brentham chief merchandising officer Luz Ferrante said the data confirmed what many in the book community have long argued: that discoverability, not demand, is the primary obstacle to translated literature finding a mainstream readership. A minimum of twenty translated titles per quarter will be stocked, chosen in consultation with a panel that includes booksellers, literary translators, and members of the Aldremian Foundation's translation advisory board. The program will be supported by in-store signage, a dedicated section on the Brentham website, and quarterly events featuring translated authors and their translators in conversation. Publishers whose titles are selected for Brentham Selects reported an average incremental sales lift of 12,000 units per title in the pilot period, representing meaningful additional revenue for publishers who have historically struggled to achieve high-street presence for translated works.

paragraph: Rosamund Bletch's second novel The Kestrel Shore, the follow-up to the Drelm-longlisted The Ferryman's Daughter, was acquired by Quill & Torrent Press in a deal described by agent Carmichael Druss as the most straightforwardly agreed transaction he had conducted in twenty-six years of practice, with no auction and a preemptive offer accepted within four hours of submission. paragraph: Memoir writer Deirdre Osholt, whose work A Geography of Leaving has sold 310,000 copies since its publication two years ago by Ardentine Books, announced a second memoir acquired by the same publisher for $880,000. The new work, titled What the Water Kept, centers on Osholt's decade-long investigation into her birth father's identity, which ultimately reveals a connection to a significant historical cover-up involving a Scandinavian fishing community in the 1950s. Agent Yusuf Brandel confirmed the deal following a controlled auction. Ardentine editor Nora Velthuijs described the manuscript, which arrives at 360 pages, as operating simultaneously as personal memoir, investigative journalism, and environmental history. Publication is set for autumn 2027 with an initial print run of 90,000, supported by a $380,000 marketing budget. Translation rights were sold in twenty-six countries at the Frankfurt Book Fair, generating $1.4 million in aggregate — comfortably exceeding the North American advance. Film rights attracted competitive interest from three production companies, with Lentivast Media ultimately securing an option for $320,000. Osholt's audio editions, narrated by the author herself, have accumulated 3.8 million streams across subscription platforms for her first memoir, creating strong appetite among audio publishers for the new title.

paragraph: Heliograph Books, the romance imprint of Fenwick & Allard, launched a dedicated new line for historical romance on Monday, titled Heliograph Historical, with an initial list of six titles scheduled between September 2026 and June 2027. The imprint will focus on romances set in non-Western historical settings, reflecting what Heliograph editorial director Britta Sander described as a persistent gap in the genre landscape. Sander confirmed that all six authors retained their own-language publication rights and that co-publication deals have been concluded with publishers in four countries. The imprint has budgeted $280,000 for its inaugural marketing campaign, structured around a reader community engagement strategy developed with social media consultant Renata Flores, who previously managed the BookTok strategy that drove Dorienne Calst's breakout success. Print runs for the first six titles range from 20,000 to 45,000 copies.

paragraph: Quill & Torrent Press reported a near-final deal to acquire world rights to the debut novel of architect and urbanist Joachim Feld for $540,000, with agent Hannelore Strang of the Berlinhal Agency negotiating the agreement. Feld's novel, The Invisible City, is a 445-page work of literary fiction that presents a fictional European metropolis through the eyes of twelve inhabitants across a single day of civic crisis, structured formally after the floor plans of the buildings in which each character lives. Quill & Torrent editorial director Ottoline Marsh described the formal conceit as justified at every level of execution, not merely a structural novelty. Publication is planned for autumn 2028 following a substantial developmental edit. Translation rights have attracted interest from publishers in France, Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands, where the novel's urban themes are expected to resonate with readers already familiar with the genre tradition of urban literary fiction. Marsh noted that the Drelm Prize committee has informally communicated enthusiasm about the manuscript, though formal eligibility will be determined only upon publication. A film option has been declined by Feld on the grounds that the formal architecture of the novel is untranslatable to screen — a stance that agent Strang confirmed the author holds firmly and that is reflected in the contract.

paragraph: Audio publisher Vellum Audio announced record revenues of $34.7 million for fiscal 2025, a 52 percent increase over the prior year, as subscription platform growth and a series of high-profile exclusive productions drove exceptional listener numbers. The company has signed original audio commissions from six established novelists including crime writer Friede Kallenbach and science-fiction author Tamsin Ruark, whose four-part audio serial set in the same fictional universe as The Solvent Dark will debut in autumn 2027. Nolde confirmed that Vellum is in advanced discussions with two streaming platforms about exclusive content agreements, which could represent a fundamental shift in the company's distribution strategy.

paragraph: The annual Aldremian Foundation Lecture, the most prestigious single event in the literary publishing calendar, was delivered this year by novelist Halberg Korov, whose remarks on the relationship between historical imagination and political responsibility in fiction drew both praise and controversy from industry observers. Speaking before an audience of 1,200 at the Aldremian Foundation Gala in Edinburgh, Korov argued that the historical novel is not an escape from the present but the most rigorous available method for examining it, and that publishers who treat historical fiction as commercial decoration rather than intellectual inquiry are failing both readers and writers. The lecture was subsequently published in full in the Aldremian Review and reprinted in eleven publications internationally. Korov took the occasion to announce that his next novel, under contract with Mendovian House for $1.05 million, will be set in present-day Lisbon and Maputo and will not be a historical work in any conventional sense — a departure that his publisher described as enormously exciting and his agent, Carmichael Druss, as a commercial risk worth embracing given the author's established literary standing. The Aldremian Foundation also announced at the Gala that it would increase the Drelm Prize cash award from £60,000 to £90,000 beginning with the 2027 cycle.

paragraph: Luuk de Mond's debut novel The Architect of Small Defeats, published by Eschenwald Publishing in March at $27.00, became the winner of the 2026 Welmsley Prize for Debut Fiction, defeating five strong competitors including Obinna Tarwell's The Unfinished Country and Seun Adegbite's A Thousand Ordinary Rivers. De Mond, a Dutch novelist and former city planner, wrote The Architect of Small Defeats — a 398-page meditation on municipal bureaucracy, personal failure, and the quiet heroism of mid-level public servants in a fictional Flemish city — while working full-time in Amsterdam's urban planning department. The £35,000 prize was accepted in a brief, characteristically understated address in which de Mond thanked his family, his editor at Eschenwald, and the city of Amsterdam for providing such rich material. Eschenwald publisher Alara Kindtmann confirmed that sales surged to 85,000 copies in the week following the announcement, supported by an emergency third printing of 60,000 copies. Translation rights, previously sold in eleven territories, have now been acquired in a further nine, bringing the total to twenty. Film rights were optioned within forty-eight hours of the prize announcement for $195,000. De Mond's second novel is already under contract with Eschenwald for $680,000.

paragraph: Vorthex & Sons reported that Seun Adegbite has signed a two-book follow-up deal worth $1.3 million following the extraordinary commercial and critical success of A Thousand Ordinary Rivers. paragraph: The Castebrook Prize for Narrative Nonfiction revealed that its endowment has been doubled to $4 million following a private donation from the Felhorn Family Foundation — no relation to Dr. Cass Felhorn — allowing the prize committee to increase the annual cash award from $30,000 to $50,000 beginning in 2027 and to introduce two new categories: a prize for narrative nonfiction in translation and a prize for debut nonfiction. The Castebrook Prize, established in 1987, is administered by the Orvallen Institute and considered one of the three most significant nonfiction prizes in the English-speaking world. Prize director Anastasia Brenner stated that the expansion reflects the growing importance of narrative nonfiction as a vehicle for public understanding of complex subjects and the need to recognize the full ecosystem of voices contributing to the form. Previous winners of the prize include Arpad Golmar, Lavinia Osei, and Deirdre Osholt, all of whose publishers reported meaningful sales increases in backlist titles following the announcement of the endowment expansion. Applications for the inaugural translated nonfiction and debut nonfiction categories will open in January 2027, with the first expanded prize ceremony scheduled for November 2027.

paragraph: Industry association the Aldremian Publishers Consortium released its annual State of the Book Trade report for 2025, revealing that total net revenues across consortium members reached $4.1 billion, a 7.2 percent increase over 2024 and the fourth consecutive year of growth following the post-pandemic normalization of 2021. Literary fiction grew 11.2 percent, driven by exceptional prize-season performance and the commercial dominance of Sonia Fethwick's The Glasspine Tribunal. Science fiction and fantasy grew 14.7 percent, reflecting the sustained strength of established series and an influx of ambitious debut titles. Audio formats grew 38 percent, representing 22 percent of total industry revenue for the first time. Physical unit sales declined by 1.3 percent, though average cover price increases of 4.8 percent more than offset the volume decline in revenue terms. Translation rights income grew 24 percent across consortium members to a combined $780 million, the largest single-year increase since the metric was first tracked in 2009. The report's authors identified the growing overlap between literary fiction and speculative fiction as the defining structural trend of the current market, noting that seven of the ten bestselling literary titles of 2025 contained speculative elements that would previously have categorized them as genre fiction.
